skill.name: file-boundaries
skill.description: Guides agents to respect explicit file ownership boundaries during parallel code work.
skill.summary: Keep parallel coding agents inside assigned file ownership boundaries.
skill.author: Clouder0

security_audit.summary: The static command, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance findings are false positives from Markdown examples and generic text. The confirmed risk is filesystem impact: the skill allows Write and Edit tools and describes file creation, modification, and deletion inside declared ownership boundaries. Publish with a clear warning that boundaries must be explicit and reviewed before use.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Modification Depends on Boundary Quality
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Write and Edit tools and tells agents they can read, modify, create, and delete owned files. This is legitimate for coding coordination, but unsafe or vague ownership lists could lead to unwanted file changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly grants write-capable tools and documents create, modify, and delete actions. The risk depends on user-provided boundaries, so it is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited locations are fenced YAML examples and inline Markdown path examples. No Ruby code, shell backtick execution, or executable command invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are documentation examples, not runnable scripts. There is no shell command, interpolation, or execution API in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines describe collaboration goals and example blocked details. No weak cryptographic algorithm, cryptographic operation, or system reconnaissance behavior appears in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text contains plain documentation and example file names only. I found no evidence of cryptographic algorithms or host information gathering.

content.value_statement: Parallel AI coding can cause conflicting edits when agents touch the same files. This skill defines ownership and read-only boundaries so Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can coordinate safely.
actual_capabilities: Defines owned files that an agent can modify.
Defines read-only files that an agent can inspect but not change.
Instructs agents to stop when a needed edit is outside scope.
Provides a blocked-report format for boundary violations.
Supports parallel work by assigning separate file areas to agents.
limitations: It does not enforce boundaries through technical access controls.
It depends on clear ownership lists from the user or coordinator.
It does not resolve merge conflicts after boundaries are changed.
It only documents a workflow and contains no automation.

use_cases.title: Split Feature Work Across Agents
use_cases.description: Assign different files to each coding agent so they can work in parallel with fewer conflicts.

use_cases.title: Protect Shared Type Files
use_cases.description: Let an agent read shared definitions while requiring approval before changing them.

use_cases.title: Report Blocked Implementation Steps
use_cases.description: Create a clear blocked message when a task needs edits outside the assigned files.

prompt_templates.title: Use Basic Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the file-boundaries skill. Only modify the files I mark as owned, and report blocked for any other needed edit.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single agent task

prompt_templates.title: Separate Owned and Read-Only Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use owned files for implementation and read-only files for reference. Do not change read-only files without a blocked report.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Parallel Agents
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply file boundaries for this parallel task. Keep your edits inside your assigned area and explain any boundary conflict.
prompt_templates.scenario: Multiple agents working in one repository

prompt_templates.title: Audit Boundary Compliance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the planned edits against the assigned file boundaries. Identify allowed edits, read-only dependencies, and blocked changes.

output_examples.input: An agent needs to update a shared user type that is read-only.
output_examples.output: The agent reports blocked and explains that the shared type file needs permission or a contract update.

output_examples.input: Two agents work on authentication and API files at the same time.
output_examples.output: Each agent stays in its assigned file area and avoids editing files owned by the other agent.

output_examples.input: A requested fix only touches files listed as owned.
output_examples.output: The agent proceeds with the implementation and keeps all changes inside the approved boundary.

best_practices: Define owned and read-only files before implementation starts.
Keep shared contracts read-only unless one agent is assigned ownership.
Review blocked reports before expanding an agent's boundary.
anti_patterns: Using broad ownership such as an entire repository without review.
Letting agents modify read-only files because the change seems small.

faq.question: Does this skill enforce file permissions?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow rules for an agent to follow. It does not create technical access controls.

faq.question: Can an agent delete files with this skill?
faq.answer: The skill allows deletion only for owned files. Review ownership carefully before allowing destructive edits.

faq.question: Is this useful for one agent?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps a single agent avoid touching shared files without approval.

faq.question: What happens when a read-only file must change?
faq.answer: The agent should stop and report blocked with the needed change and suggested resolution.

faq.question: Does it include code or scripts?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a Markdown workflow guide with examples.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
